executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

National Medicaid enrollment shows a concerning downward trajectory as of January 2026, with total enrollment at 75.3 million representing widespread year-over-year declines across major states. The most significant drops occurred in Michigan (-7.6%) and California and Illinois (both -6.2%), while only North Carolina posted enrollment growth (+0.5%). Texas experienced a moderate decline of 3.1% to approximately 4.0 million enrollees, positioning it as the third-largest Medicaid program nationally. These enrollment reductions likely reflect the ongoing unwinding of pandemic-era continuous coverage protections, requiring careful monitoring to ensure eligible populations maintain access to coverage.

Spending patterns reveal substantial variation in per-enrollee costs across states, with the national average at $2,869 in fiscal year 2024. Pennsylvania leads with $4,377 per enrollee, followed by Massachusetts at $4,201, while Florida demonstrates the lowest per-enrollee spending at $2,031. Texas falls near the national average at $2,988 per enrollee despite having the fourth-highest total expenditures at $12.0 billion. Quality performance presents additional challenges, with the national average score of 65.6 out of 100 indicating significant room for improvement. Texas ranks 30th nationally with a score of 62, placing it in the moderate performance tier but below states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80). Notably, Texas demonstrates strong managed care penetration at 93.3%, exceeding the national average of 83.2%, which positions the state well for coordinated care delivery and cost management initiatives.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing a significant but proportionally smaller coverage footprint compared to peer states. At $2,988 per enrollee, Texas spends modestly above the national average of $2,869 but substantially below expansion states like New York ($3,431) and Pennsylvania ($4,377). This positions Texas favorably among large non-expansion states, spending more efficiently than Ohio ($3,561) while maintaining higher enrollment than Georgia (1.85 million). The state's 3.1% year-over-year enrollment decline aligns with national post-pandemic normalization trends, tracking similarly to Pennsylvania but outperforming the steep drops seen in California (-6.2%) and Michigan (-7.6%). Texas's total program expenditure of $12.0 billion reflects disciplined cost management relative to its enrollment scale.

However, quality performance remains Texas's most pressing challenge, with a score of 62/100 ranking 30th nationally and placing it in the bottom tier among large states. This moderate performance significantly lags behind top-performing large states and falls closer to struggling Southern peers like Arkansas (54) and South Carolina (53), suggesting systemic regional challenges in care delivery and outcomes measurement. The state's strength lies in managed care penetration at 93.3%, well above the 83.2% national average, indicating sophisticated care coordination infrastructure that could serve as a foundation for quality improvements. As Texas faces ongoing federal regulatory changes around HCBS quality measures and interoperability standards, the state's ability to leverage its managed care platform while addressing quality gaps will be critical for maintaining federal compliance and improving beneficiary outcomes in this large, complex program.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost control issues.

alerts.title: Michigan Medicaid Enrollment Drops 7.6% Year-Over-Year
alerts.description: Michigan experienced a significant enrollment decline of 7.6% compared to last year, well above the 10% threshold for concern.

alerts.title: Florida Shows Unusually Low Per-Enrollee Spending
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average, potentially indicating access or adequacy issues.

alerts.title: Five States Score Below 55 on Quality Measures
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi all scored below 55 on quality measures, indicating serious care quality concerns.

alerts.title: Texas: Moderate Quality Despite High Managed Care Penetration
alerts.description: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration (above national 83.2%) but only moderate quality scores (62/100), suggesting efficiency opportunities.

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score significantly below national average at #30 ranking
executiveInsights.summary: Texas scored 62/100 on quality measures, ranking #30 out of 50 states and falling below the national average of 65.6. This places Texas in the bottom 40% of states for Medicaid quality performance.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality performance directly impacts contract renewals and federal oversight scrutiny. Poor rankings could trigger enhanced federal monitoring or contract modifications.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement initiatives to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: New HCBS quality measures for 2028 require immediate preparation
executiveInsights.summary: CMS has published new Home and Community-Based Services quality measure requirements for 2028. These regulatory changes will impact how Texas Medicaid quality is assessed and reported.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New quality measures require system modifications and process changes that take 12-18 months to implement. Early preparation is critical to avoid compliance gaps.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review Federal Register HCBS quality measure requirements with compliance team

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment decline of 3.1% aligns with national unwinding trend
executiveInsights.summary: Texas lost 3.1% of Medicaid enrollees year-over-year, consistent with national post-pandemic unwinding patterns. With 4.03M enrollees, Texas remains the 3rd largest Medicaid program.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment declines impact contract revenue and may require workforce adjustments or service delivery modifications. Understanding the trend helps forecast future capacity needs.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review capacity planning assumptions with HHSC based on enrollment trends

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs above national average at $2,988 vs $2,869
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee compared to the national average of $2,869. Despite high managed care penetration at 93.3%, costs remain elevated.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs indicate potential inefficiencies or cost management opportunities. This creates pressure for value-based initiatives and cost containment strategies.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag cost benchmarking analysis to HHSC finance team

executiveInsights.title: New interoperability and prior authorization standards published
executiveInsights.summary: CMS has issued new interoperability standards and prior authorization requirements that will affect Texas Medicaid operations. These regulations mandate system and process changes.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Interoperability requirements affect IT infrastructure and data exchange capabilities, requiring technical compliance investments and timeline coordination.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review interoperability requirements with IT and compliance teams

riskOpportunity.label: Texas quality ranking improvement
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating pressure for system modernization and performance enhancement initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop comprehensive quality improvement roadmap targeting specific CMS measures where Texas underperforms.

riskOpportunity.label: Medicaid enrollment decline
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY to 4.03M, indicating redetermination impacts and potential operational strain.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose enrollment stabilization and member retention analytics services to HHSC.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality measures
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Home and Community-Based Services quality measure requirements will demand significant system and reporting upgrades.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position Accenture as lead implementer for HCBS quality measurement system transformation.

riskOpportunity.label: HHS OIG oversight
riskOpportunity.description: Active OIG oversight docket and work plan items create compliance risk but also opportunity for remediation contracts.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Proactively monitor OIG findings and develop compliance remediation service offerings.

riskOpportunity.label: Interoperability standards compliance
riskOpportunity.description: New Medicare and Medicaid interoperability and prior authorization standards require significant technical infrastructure updates.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Lead HHSC interoperability transformation initiative to ensure compliance and improve data exchange.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care optimization
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's 93.3% managed care penetration above national average presents opportunities for advanced MCO analytics and oversight.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop enhanced MCO performance monitoring and value-based care analytics capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: HHSC procurement pipeline
riskOpportunity.description: Active Texas HHSC procurement opportunities portal indicates ongoing competitive landscape for technology and consulting services.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish dedicated HHSC procurement tracking and rapid response capability.

riskOpportunity.label: Per-enrollee cost efficiency
riskOpportunity.description: Texas's $2,988 per-enrollee cost is above national average, creating pressure for cost reduction and efficiency initiatives.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose comprehensive cost optimization analysis and implementation services to demonstrate ROI.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 4,032,864 Medicaid enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee annually in fiscal year 2024.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 managed care enrollees.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas ranks 30th of 50 states with a moderate quality score of 62 out of 100.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active Texas procurement portals are being monitored for Medicaid opportunities.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Six federal regulatory and OIG items are currently active affecting Medicaid programs.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific milestone dates are available in the current data.
